sentences.text: I can still move?
It shows already?
It's taping me?
OK.
Just here.
Yeah, I know.
Do I have my address there?
Sorry.
I was born in Hajdunanas, Hungary.
Debrecen.
It was close to Debrecen.
That was the capital of the region, yes.
Yes.
My father was born in 1906.
I don't know the month.
And my mother, 1904.
They were both born in Hajdunanas, and they were married there.
And what I remember from my father, the first picture, in-- that was in 1942.
And I remember that he was going away, and I was crying.
And I told my mother not to let him go.
And the second time I remember him, we were already in the ghetto.
When we were in the ghetto, he-- I'm sorry.
We weren't in the ghetto.
We weren't in the ghetto yet.
But he came back to see us.
He escaped wherever he was in Hungary, and he came to see us.
But he-- we were-- at that time, we were not allowed to go, any time of the day or night, out.
That's how I remember.
Because we went to-- my father and my mother and the children went to visit my mother's parents.
And it was getting dark, and the grandmother-- my grandmother says to go already, go, you know, [INAUDIBLE], that we shouldn't be going in the dark.
We should get back to our home so we shouldn't get into trouble.
This was the second time I remember him.
I-- After that, I never seen him.
We are four children.
And a mother and a father.
I'm the youngest child, yeah.
It was hardworking people.
Most of them weren't well to do at all.
They had to work very, very hard to make a living.
And they were busy with their-- just making a living and looking after their families.
They minded their own business.
And--
I don't know how-- wasn't a very huge, maybe 3,000, but I'm not sure of it.
Well, before the war, I-- I really remember that I was always among my family-- with my grandparents, uncles, and aunts.
And-- but where we lived, where there-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not just-- not only Jewish people lived on the street.
We had other neighbors too.
Well, Hajdunanas is not a very big place.
And there was a Jewish street, where the synagogue and the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the cheder was there.
And all the really activities was in this.
Where the synagogue was, it was a very big place.
Must have been a few acres.
And there was Jewish school, and there was the cheder, and there was the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the [INAUDIBLE] lived on-- on that-- they had a house there.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the shamas-- I don't know how you say that in English-- was there.
And everything was-- actually was taking place in this area.
And on this street, a lot of Jews lived.
But there was a-- there was a couple, a few non-Jews.
My immediate family, my parents?
No.
We didn't live on that street.
With the neighbors?
Well, we always said hello, good morning, good evening.
And it wasn't bad.
It was-- before the war, I don't remember.
It was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't remember too much over there.
I only remember that I was just walking and-- and the dogs were barking, because everybody had a dog there.
And I just went to my grandparents.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
Well, I had both grandparents.
I had my father's parents, and I had my mother's parents.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] one-- one grandparents, they actually lived in an area where there were-- it was a non-Jewish area.
That was my father's parents.
Yes.
And--
Yes.
My grandfather, he was a Hebrew teacher.
And my grandmother, she was-- she was a very smart, independent woman-- strong woman.

And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] she-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] she-- she was doing all kind of ways to-- to-- to make their liv-- their financial [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] life better, because the teaching didn't pay much.
And a lot of people sent their children to the cheder, but not everybody was paying, not-- because they didn't have money to pay, not everybody had.
So my grandmother was very creative, and she-- she was-- she was making some money also, so this way they were all right.
What she was-- not exactly what she was selling.
But--
She was helping, yes, the income.
The same town, yes.
And--
Well, my grandmother was very sick.
When I remember her, she was already in bed.
She was a sick woman.
She was on medication.
She had to have medication.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my mother was helping a lot to raise the youngest children, because my grandmother's illness.
Yes, the oldest child for my grandmother, yes.
The youngest ones, yes, yeah.
Especially the youngest sister.
Yes.
And, well, they all-- they were all working.
The grandfather and my uncle and the-- my aunts, they were all-- I remember they have sewing machines in the room there.
And my grandfather was cutting, and everybody else was sewing on the machine.
And that's how they made a living.
Yes, very close, to both of them.
Both of them.
Well, I don't remember get-togethers so much.
But I just remember that-- that I loved them very much.
[CRYING] Sorry.
And that they loved us very much.
So wherever we went, or to the Safar grandparents or to the Rosenberg grandparents, we were equally welcome and we were very, very much loved.
And if you would ask me which one I loved more, I loved one of them more than the other one, I don't know which one was-- I loved more, because they were-- I just adored them.
You were the baby.
I was the baby.
And from the Safar side-- from my mother's side-- we were the only grandchildren to my grandparents.
And to my other side of the grand-- my father's side of the grandparents, we were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 13 grandchildren.
And among the 13 grandchildren, only we are four of us survived.
Everybody else was killed in Auschwitz with their mothers.
With their mothers.
The family had been in Hajdunanas for many, many years.
For a very long time, many generations.
We had a great-great-grandfather, my grandfather's [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] grandfather, it was, on the Safar side.
And he lived to be 106 years old.
And he died in Hajdunanas and he's buried there.
And he had children of course, and the grandchildren, and us, so quite a few generations.
Well, my immediate, my father and mother?
Well, the grandparents, they had to work very hard to make the living and, you know, to support themselves.
And everybody else.
My father was working very hard.
And by all means we were not well-to-do at all.
But my father provided that we had food and clothes and we rented an apartment.
And we didn't feel that we missed anything.
He was a salesman.
Yes.
He was-- in Hajdunanas, there was a sort of an industry, like straw.
People were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] knitting-- I think that's the word for it, knitting-- straw.
Wider ones, narrower ones, thicker ones, thinner ones.
And from those straw-- they were doing it by the meter, and they were selling it by the meter.
And this is what women in the evenings-- they were sitting outside and were talking, socializing-- and their hands were going and they were knitting it.
And from those straws, they were making hats, bags, slippers.
They were even making little mats.
And he was traveling with this and he was selling it.
He was also selling, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember, candies.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A lot of other things.
I don't remember the other things, but the candies I remember.
Well, I remember my father coming back-- because he only came home for the weekends, was traveling all week.
And he likes to tease us children.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] we had a-- we had a very big-- in this-- in this room, we had a bedroom, which was having two big cupboards, linen closets.
And one was hanging clothes, and it was very tall.
And it was against the wall, I remember.
And the bed was close to it.
And it had a headboard and it had a footboard also.
So because I was the youngest, and my siblings were telling me that I should stand up there, because top of the cupboard, there are candies.
There were candy which, like-- like flat candies.
We can-- and a knife was on the top.
And you have to cut it to get a piece from it.
And our father was-- he never really hit us.
He was very strict.
He only had to just look at us and we already knew that we had to be-- we had to behave ourselves.
But to make sure that nobody should get into trouble, they told me I should go up there and start-- and start to cut candies.
And I got up there and I even had to go a little bit on the tippy-toe because I was very little.
And I started to cut the candy.
And my siblings said, cut more, cut more, cut more.
And I gave for everybody.
And then I came down.
And there was big excitement father shouldn't walk in.
He didn't walk in.
And we got our candy and then we were all happy.
After the war, I mentioned this to my mother.
And I asked her, why did father do this?
I said, why didn't he just gave it to us?
He says, he-- because he told us, don't touch the candy, when he put it up.
And he said-- she said to me, he specially brought home that piece of candy for the children.
And he put it up there with the knife that you should get it that way.
He just liked to tease you like that.
But it was for you children.
This-- this is what I remember that he did.
Well, I was-- at that time, I was so young.
It's-- the discipline, it was the same for all us.
It's just-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was-- oh, one of my brothers, he was mischievous.
He was a mischievous boy.
But at home, he was an angel.
And at home, he always helped my mother and he was very good.
He was a very good boy.
He's a very good person.
And when my father came home from his traveling and he went to the synagogue, and he would have liked to pray and socialize a little bit afterwards.
But he couldn't, because constantly they were telling, Mr.
Rosenberg, your son did this.
Your son did that.
And he got angry, and he was coming home.
And he said to my mother, this time I'm going to-- I'm going to punish him, because they complain so much.
So he looks toward my brother and he's helping my mother.
He's talking nice.
He's polite.
So he-- what can he do with a boy who front of him his-- he never misbehaves?
So he just turned away and he started to smile.
And he said to my mother, I cannot.
I cannot do anything, because he's good.
How can I punish a good child?
So that's the way he was.
He did not-- physically, he never-- he loved us too much.
Perhaps he felt the time was short.
Who knows?
Was the family religious?
Yes.
We were religious, yes.
Well, before the war, I don't--
All I know that we were religious.
We were orthodox.
Well, it's-- the shul, yes.
The shul it was.
And the-- just very close-knit and it's-- very plain, it was, actually.
Nothing really exciting was happening, just everyday living, trying to survive.
Five.
Yes.
Yes.
And I remember that I was in-- I was-- I was in pain.
It was hurting me that he was leaving.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- but he had to.
All the young men were taken in 1942.
Only the old people and the young.
Only the old male and the young male, the children, stayed behind.
No.
No.
No.
No.
After the war, when we came back, we-- well, we were always hoping that he's coming back, because that's what my mother said, that-- all the time to us, that father is waiting for us at home.
And I had a hope that he's there.
And I even had a picture in my mind that he's holding the doorknob.
And I said to my mother, he can't open the door.
He hasn't got a key.
So my mother says, don't worry.
He will open the door.
But I see him-- even today I can see him as the way I pictured him that he's holding the knob and he's-- he wants to go-- he wants to open it.
And when we came back from the war, my mother didn't let us go for Yizkor for many years.
When in the synagogue, the high holidays, they were the-- Yizkor, she says-- she always send us out.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I asked.
I said, well, why can't we stay?
She says, not-- she says, not yet.
Maybe, because they were hearing-- we could hear-- we heard that some men came back.
They were far and they were for many years after the war they didn't return, and then they just showed up.
So my mother says, you see, your father will come too.
Because he was a man who was a very capable young man.
And after the war, when I was in Debrecen visiting my aunt, and I was standing in her apartment doorway, because this was a courtyard.
Other people lived there too.
And a man who I never seen before, and he never seen me before.
And I'm standing there and he's walking towards me.
And he says, are you Joseph Rosenberg's daughter?
And I got all excited.
I said, yes, I am.
I said, how would you know that I am his daughter?
He says, well, you look like-- just like him.
It's the same face.
So I said, if you recognize me, I said, from my father, so where is my father?
How do you know him?
So he says they were together at one part of-- in the war.
And I said, well, where is he?
I said, you came back.
Where is he?

So he says, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I tell you, if your father would be alive, he would-- he would be here, because he was a man, if he would have been locked in a room where there were no doors and no windows, he would have been able to come out from that room and save himself.
He was helping people, his comrade there-- his fellow inmates, I should say.
And he was saying-- talking very nice about him.
[PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] [CRYING]
So after a few years passes and there is another Yizkor on a high holiday and I'm ready to go out and my mother says, you can stay.
[SOBBING] So then I knew that I have no more father.
There's no more hope.
He's gone.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
And you know what with this feeling, what else comes?
Even anger came.
Why is he not back?
[SOBBING] Why isn't he with us?
[SOBBING] So some people used to appear who had been away for some time and that's what kept the-- the belief?
We believed.
I believed my mother.
She believed it.
She believed it so strong that he's going to come back.
[SOBBING] You see, he was very good to her.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was always so good.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We always relied on him.
My grandparents, my mother's parents, adored him.
Her sisters, her brothers adored him.
He was a very good person.
He was young.
He was healthy.
Nothing wrong with him.
His problem was that he was born Jewish, [SOBBING] so he was killed.
One man came back-- this was the only one who came back to Hajdunanas who was with my father.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this man-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he was-- he was a ter-- he was almost a nervous wreck already.
He was a very nice man, but you could tell that he went through a lot.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So once he told us [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that they were marching in the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was in the Ukraine, I guess, in the [INAUDIBLE], the [INAUDIBLE] river.
That's what he said.
And they were walking and walking, and suddenly the bombs was falling.
And he said, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when he got up from the floor-- from the dirt, he says nobody was there.
Nobody.
The earth swallowed them.
That's-- that's what we know about him.
And there was my uncle, my mother's brother was there also with my father, Moishe Safar.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were the same age.
So the earth swallowed them.
[NON-ENGLISH] It is a labor battalion, I think.
I think so, yes.
Because in '42, that's where they took them.
That's where they-- yes.
And so they had been sent to the front and-- where the bombs had--
I don't know the details.
This is how much I know, and that he didn't survive.
Some-- and this man said that they suffered a lot.
They were put through an awful lot, and it was very terrible.
But he did not want to talk more about it.
He said it was just terrible.
I guess he couldn't talk.
I don't know.
I was just a teenager.
I don't know.
I don't know which year this was.
I don't know which year this was.
But I remember, in the synagogue, it was so empty.
It was a very beautiful synagogue, beautiful, big.
And it really was a terrible, empty place on the high holidays.
Nothing, just a few rows on the front.
Maybe two rows on the ladies' side.
Upstairs were the ladies.
And downstairs maybe three rows of men.
And the rest of it was empty, empty, empty.
And hundreds of people were able to go in there before.
Thank you.
